questions.questionText: Pregnancy Risk Factors - Biophysical
Pregnancy Risk Factors - Psychosocial
Pregnancy Risk Factors - Sociodemographic
Pregnancy Risk Factors - Environmental
Beginning at 28 weeks, fewer than ___ fetal kick counts in 2 hours warrants further investigation
Non-stress test (NST), performed after 28 weeks:
options: Reactive is 2 accelerations, Non-reactive did not meet criteria
questions.correctAnswer: Reactive is 2 accelerations, Non-reactive did not meet criteria

questions.questionText: Contraction Stress Test (CST), after 34 weeks, evaluate FHR for decelerations
options: Negative: No decelerations with contractions. Positive: Late decelerations with 50% or more contractions. Equivocal: Decelerations are intermittent.
questions.correctAnswer: Negative: No decelerations with contractions. Positive: Late decelerations with 50% or more contractions. Equivocal: Decelerations are intermittent.

questions.questionText: Ultrasound - First Trimester
options: Confirm pregnancy & viability, determine uterine abnormalities.
Confirm due date, anatomy scan @ 20 weeks, detect anomalies, measure growth, determine placental location.
Measure fetal growth, assess placental functioning, determine fetal presenting part, determine placental location, perform doppler flow studies.
questions.correctAnswer: Confirm pregnancy & viability, determine uterine abnormalities.

questions.questionText: Ultrasound - Second Trimester
options: Confirm pregnancy & viability, determine uterine abnormalities.
Confirm due date, anatomy scan @ 20 weeks, detect anomalies, measure growth, determine placental location.
Measure fetal growth, assess placental functioning, determine fetal presenting part, determine placental location, perform doppler flow studies.
questions.correctAnswer: Confirm due date, anatomy scan @ 20 weeks, detect anomalies, measure growth, determine placental location.

questions.questionText: Ultrasound - Third Trimester
options: Confirm pregnancy & viability, determine uterine abnormalities.
Confirm due date, anatomy scan @ 20 weeks, detect anomalies, measure growth, determine placental location.
Measure fetal growth, assess placental functioning, determine fetal presenting part, determine placental location, perform doppler flow studies.
questions.correctAnswer: Measure fetal growth, assess placental functioning, determine fetal presenting part, determine placental location, perform doppler flow studies.

questions.questionText: Genetic Disorders - Chromosomal
options: Sickle Cell Anemia, Cystic Fibrosis, Huntington’s Disease
Neural tube defect (spina bifida), diabetes, cancer

questions.questionText: Genetic Disorders - Single-gene
options: Sickle Cell Anemia, Cystic Fibrosis, Huntington’s Disease
Neural tube defect (spina bifida), diabetes, cancer
questions.correctAnswer: Sickle Cell Anemia, Cystic Fibrosis, Huntington’s Disease

questions.questionText: Genetic Disorders - Complex
options: Sickle Cell Anemia, Cystic Fibrosis, Huntington’s Disease
Neural tube defect (spina bifida), diabetes, cancer
questions.correctAnswer: Neural tube defect (spina bifida), diabetes, cancer

questions.questionText: Down Syndrome
options: Life expectancy is 47 years old.  Associated with Congenital heart defects, Hearing loss, Obstructive sleep apnea, Ear infections, Eye disease/need for glasses
questions.correctAnswer: Life expectancy is 47 years old.  Associated with Congenital heart defects, Hearing loss, Obstructive sleep apnea, Ear infections, Eye disease/need for glasses

questions.questionText: Autosomal Inheritance Disorders - Dominant
options: Huntington’s Disease, Marfan Syndrome;  Only requires ONE abnormal gene to have disorder;  If one parent passes down the disease, offspring has 50% chance of inheritance

Sickle Cell Anemia, Cystic Fibrosis,  Tay-Sachs Disease;  Both genes must be abnormal to have disorder;  One affected gene = carrier;  If both parents are carriers, offspring has 25% chance of inheritance
questions.correctAnswer: Huntington’s Disease, Marfan Syndrome;  Only requires ONE abnormal gene to have disorder;  If one parent passes down the disease, offspring has 50% chance of inheritance

questions.questionText: Autosomal Inheritance Disorders - Recessive
options: Huntington’s Disease, Marfan Syndrome;  Only requires ONE abnormal gene to have disorder;  If one parent passes down the disease, offspring has 50% chance of inheritance

Sickle Cell Anemia, Cystic Fibrosis,  Tay-Sachs Disease;  Both genes must be abnormal to have disorder;  One affected gene = carrier;  If both parents are carriers, offspring has 25% chance of inheritance
questions.correctAnswer: Sickle Cell Anemia, Cystic Fibrosis,  Tay-Sachs Disease;  Both genes must be abnormal to have disorder;  One affected gene = carrier;  If both parents are carriers, offspring has 25% chance of inheritance

questions.questionText: Anna asks if her baby has Down syndrome. How can the nurse explain “soft markers” to Anna?
options: Soft markers are ultrasound findings that are sometimes seen in babies with Down syndrome, but they can also be found in many healthy babies. Soft markers are not birth defects and do not diagnose Down syndrome. They simply indicate that there may be a slightly increased chance that the baby has a chromosome condition.  Examples: Thickened nuchal fold, Echogenic bowel, Echogenic intracardiac focus, Short femur or humerus, Mild kidney dilation
questions.correctAnswer: Soft markers are ultrasound findings that are sometimes seen in babies with Down syndrome, but they can also be found in many healthy babies. Soft markers are not birth defects and do not diagnose Down syndrome. They simply indicate that there may be a slightly increased chance that the baby has a chromosome condition.  Examples: Thickened nuchal fold, Echogenic bowel, Echogenic intracardiac focus, Short femur or humerus, Mild kidney dilation

questions.questionText: What counseling should Anna receive about amniocentesis?
options: The nurse should explain that amniocentesis is a diagnostic procedure that uses a sample of amniotic fluid to analyze the baby's chromosomes and can accurately determine whether conditions such as Down syndrome are present. The procedure is typically performed after 15 weeks of pregnancy using ultrasound guidance. Counseling should include discussion of the benefits of obtaining definitive genetic information, as well as the risks, which include a small chance of miscarriage, infection, bleeding, cramping, leakage of amniotic fluid, and Rh sensitization in Rh-negative mothers. The nurse should provide unbiased information, answer questions, and support Anna's decision-making process.
questions.correctAnswer: The nurse should explain that amniocentesis is a diagnostic procedure that uses a sample of amniotic fluid to analyze the baby's chromosomes and can accurately determine whether conditions such as Down syndrome are present. The procedure is typically performed after 15 weeks of pregnancy using ultrasound guidance. Counseling should include discussion of the benefits of obtaining definitive genetic information, as well as the risks, which include a small chance of miscarriage, infection, bleeding, cramping, leakage of amniotic fluid, and Rh sensitization in Rh-negative mothers. The nurse should provide unbiased information, answer questions, and support Anna's decision-making process.

questions.questionText: What are the nursing implications of genetic amniocentesis?
options: Before the procedure, the nurse should verify informed consent, assess maternal vital signs and fetal heart rate, review Rh status, explain the procedure, and provide emotional support. During the procedure, the nurse assists with positioning, maintains sterile technique, supports the patient, and monitors for discomfort while the provider obtains the amniotic fluid sample under ultrasound guidance. After the procedure, the nurse monitors maternal vital signs and fetal heart rate, assesses for vaginal bleeding, leakage of amniotic fluid, contractions, or abdominal pain, administers RhoGAM if indicated, and teaches the patient to avoid strenuous activity and report signs of complications such as fever, severe pain, bleeding, or fluid leakage.
questions.correctAnswer: Before the procedure, the nurse should verify informed consent, assess maternal vital signs and fetal heart rate, review Rh status, explain the procedure, and provide emotional support. During the procedure, the nurse assists with positioning, maintains sterile technique, supports the patient, and monitors for discomfort while the provider obtains the amniotic fluid sample under ultrasound guidance. After the procedure, the nurse monitors maternal vital signs and fetal heart rate, assesses for vaginal bleeding, leakage of amniotic fluid, contractions, or abdominal pain, administers RhoGAM if indicated, and teaches the patient to avoid strenuous activity and report signs of complications such as fever, severe pain, bleeding, or fluid leakage.

questions.questionText: How can the nurse help Anna at this time in her pregnancy?
options: The nurse can support Anna by providing emotional support, allowing her to express her feelings and concerns, and offering accurate information about Down syndrome and its potential effects on growth, development, and health. The nurse should help coordinate referrals to genetic counselors, maternal-fetal medicine specialists, pediatric specialists, and community support groups, while encouraging regular prenatal care and any recommended additional testing. Providing educational resources and helping Anna and her family prepare for the birth and future care needs of a child with Down syndrome can promote informed decision-making and reduce anxiety throughout the remainder of the pregnancy.
questions.correctAnswer: The nurse can support Anna by providing emotional support, allowing her to express her feelings and concerns, and offering accurate information about Down syndrome and its potential effects on growth, development, and health. The nurse should help coordinate referrals to genetic counselors, maternal-fetal medicine specialists, pediatric specialists, and community support groups, while encouraging regular prenatal care and any recommended additional testing. Providing educational resources and helping Anna and her family prepare for the birth and future care needs of a child with Down syndrome can promote informed decision-making and reduce anxiety throughout the remainder of the pregnancy.

questions.questionText: Is fetal kick counting considered a screening test or a diagnostic test?
questions.explanationText: Fetal kick counts are a screening method used to assess fetal well-being by monitoring fetal movement patterns. Decreased movement may indicate the need for further evaluation.

questions.questionText: When are fetal kick counts typically started?
questions.explanationText: Fetal kick counts are usually started around 28 weeks gestation and continued throughout the third trimester.

questions.questionText: Is a Non-Stress Test (NST) considered a screening test or a diagnostic test?
questions.explanationText: An NST is a screening test that evaluates fetal heart rate response to fetal movement.

questions.questionText: What does a reactive Non-Stress Test (NST) indicate?
questions.explanationText: A reactive NST indicates that the fetus is receiving adequate oxygen and is generally healthy.

questions.questionText: What is the primary purpose of an ultrasound during pregnancy?
questions.explanationText: Ultrasound uses sound waves to assess fetal growth, anatomy, placental location, and amniotic fluid levels.

questions.questionText: What two assessments are combined in a Biophysical Profile (BPP)?
questions.explanationText: A BPP combines an NST with ultrasound assessment of fetal movement, tone, breathing, and amniotic fluid.

questions.questionText: During which weeks is nuchal translucency screening typically performed?
questions.explanationText: Nuchal translucency screening is performed during the first trimester between 11 and 14 weeks gestation.

questions.questionText: Nuchal translucency screening helps assess the risk for which condition?
options: Down syndrome and other chromosomal abnormalities
questions.correctAnswer: Down syndrome and other chromosomal abnormalities
questions.explanationText: An increased nuchal translucency measurement may indicate an increased risk for chromosomal abnormalities such as Down syndrome.

questions.questionText: During which trimester is the Triple or Quad Screen typically performed?
questions.explanationText: The Triple or Quad Screen is typically performed between 15 and 22 weeks gestation during the second trimester.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the Triple or Quad Screen?
options: Estimate risk for chromosomal abnormalities and neural tube defects
questions.correctAnswer: Estimate risk for chromosomal abnormalities and neural tube defects
questions.explanationText: The Triple or Quad Screen is a maternal blood test used to estimate the risk of chromosomal abnormalities and neural tube defects.

questions.questionText: At what gestational age can cell-free DNA (cfDNA) testing be performed?
questions.explanationText: Cell-free DNA testing can be performed after 10 weeks gestation using a maternal blood sample.

questions.questionText: Is cell-free DNA (cfDNA) testing a screening test or a diagnostic test?
questions.explanationText: Cell-free DNA testing is a highly accurate screening test but does not provide a definitive diagnosis.

questions.questionText: Chorionic Villus Sampling (CVS) is classified as which type of test?
questions.explanationText: CVS is a diagnostic test that analyzes placental tissue to identify genetic and chromosomal disorders.

questions.questionText: When is Chorionic Villus Sampling (CVS) typically performed?
questions.explanationText: CVS is usually performed during the first trimester between 10 and 13 weeks gestation.

questions.questionText: Amniocentesis is classified as which type of prenatal test?
questions.explanationText: Amniocentesis is a diagnostic test that analyzes amniotic fluid for genetic and chromosomal abnormalities.

questions.questionText: When is amniocentesis typically performed?
questions.explanationText: Amniocentesis is most commonly performed between 15 and 20 weeks gestation.

questions.questionText: What is an important role of the nurse in genetic and prenatal testing?
options: Making testing decisions for the patient
questions.explanationText: The nurse educates patients about testing options, explains screening versus diagnostic tests, provides emotional support, and promotes informed decision-making.

questions.questionText: What is the primary difference between pregestational diabetes and gestational diabetes?
options: Pregestational diabetes is present before pregnancy
questions.correctAnswer: Pregestational diabetes is present before pregnancy
questions.explanationText: Pregestational diabetes refers to Type 1 or Type 2 diabetes that exists before conception, while gestational diabetes develops during pregnancy.

questions.questionText: What causes Type 1 diabetes mellitus?
options: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells
questions.correctAnswer: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells
questions.explanationText: Type 1 diabetes occurs when the body's immune system destroys insulin-producing beta cells in the pancreas.

questions.questionText: What is the primary pathogenesis of Type 2 diabetes mellitus?
options: Insulin resistance with impaired insulin secretion
questions.correctAnswer: Insulin resistance with impaired insulin secretion
questions.explanationText: Type 2 diabetes is characterized by insulin resistance and a progressive decline in insulin production.

questions.questionText: What causes gestational diabetes?
questions.explanationText: Hormones produced by the placenta increase insulin resistance, making it difficult for the mother's body to maintain normal blood glucose levels.

questions.questionText: Gestational diabetes most commonly develops during which trimester?
questions.explanationText: Gestational diabetes usually develops during the second or third trimester as placental hormone levels increase.

questions.questionText: How is gestational diabetes commonly screened for during pregnancy?
questions.explanationText: Most pregnant patients are screened with a 1-hour glucose challenge test between 24 and 28 weeks gestation.

questions.questionText: At what gestational age is screening for gestational diabetes typically performed?
questions.explanationText: Routine screening for gestational diabetes is generally performed between 24 and 28 weeks gestation.

questions.questionText: What is usually the first-line treatment for gestational diabetes?
questions.explanationText: Most patients with gestational diabetes are initially managed with nutritional counseling, glucose monitoring, and exercise.

questions.questionText: If blood glucose remains elevated despite diet and exercise, what treatment may be required?
questions.explanationText: Insulin is commonly prescribed when lifestyle modifications are insufficient to maintain target glucose levels.

questions.questionText: Which healthcare professional commonly provides nutritional counseling for pregnant patients with diabetes?
questions.explanationText: A registered dietitian is an important member of the interdisciplinary team and helps develop individualized meal plans.

questions.questionText: Which fetal complication is associated with poorly controlled maternal diabetes?
questions.explanationText: Maternal hyperglycemia can cause excessive fetal growth (macrosomia), increasing the risk of birth complications.

questions.questionText: Why are fetal kick counts important in pregnancies complicated by diabetes?
questions.explanationText: Fetal kick counts help monitor fetal activity and may identify potential problems requiring further evaluation.

questions.questionText: Which prenatal assessment may be performed regularly in diabetic pregnancies to evaluate fetal heart rate patterns?
questions.explanationText: NSTs are commonly used to assess fetal well-being by evaluating heart rate accelerations with fetal movement.

questions.questionText: Which prenatal assessment combines an NST with ultrasound evaluation of fetal movement, breathing, tone, and amniotic fluid?
questions.explanationText: A Biophysical Profile (BPP) evaluates several indicators of fetal health and is often used in high-risk pregnancies such as diabetes.

questions.questionText: Pregnant patients with pregestational diabetes are at increased risk for which congenital abnormalities?
questions.explanationText: Poor glucose control during early pregnancy increases the risk of congenital malformations, especially cardiac and neural tube defects.

questions.questionText: Which member of the healthcare team may help manage insulin therapy during pregnancy?
questions.explanationText: An endocrinologist may work with the obstetric team to optimize blood glucose management during pregnancy.

questions.questionText: What is the primary goal of diabetes management during pregnancy?
questions.explanationText: Maintaining blood glucose within target ranges helps reduce maternal and fetal complications associated with diabetes.

questions.questionText: A client with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes mellitus is seeking preconception counseling. Before actively trying to become pregnant, the client is strongly encouraged to stabilize her blood glucose to reduce the possibility of her baby developing which complication?
questions.explanationText: Poor glycemic control before conception and during early pregnancy increases the risk of congenital malformations, particularly cardiac defects and neural tube defects.

questions.questionText: Why is preconception blood glucose control important for clients with diabetes?
options: To reduce the risk of congenital malformations
To eliminate the need for prenatal care
questions.correctAnswer: To reduce the risk of congenital malformations
questions.explanationText: Tight glucose control before conception and during early pregnancy significantly reduces the risk of birth defects.

questions.questionText: Which laboratory value best reflects blood glucose control over the previous 2 to 3 months?
questions.explanationText: HbA1c provides an average of blood glucose levels over approximately 2 to 3 months and is commonly used to assess diabetic control.

questions.questionText: Which assessment can the nurse anticipate the provider will monitor to reduce the risk of fetal cardiac defects related to maternal diabetes?
questions.explanationText: HbA1c levels help determine long-term glucose control and are closely monitored before and during pregnancy.

questions.questionText: Which assessment can the nurse anticipate the provider will monitor to reduce the risk of fetal complications associated with maternal diabetes?
questions.explanationText: Monitoring blood glucose levels before meals helps ensure adequate glycemic control and reduces fetal risk.

questions.questionText: A nurse is reviewing assessments used to reduce the risk of congenital anomalies in a client with diabetes. Which two should be monitored? Select two.
questions.explanationText: Monitoring HbA1c and pre-prandial blood sugars helps evaluate and maintain optimal glucose control before and during pregnancy.

questions.questionText: Which intervention should the nurse recommend to help reduce the risk of fetal complications associated with maternal diabetes?
questions.explanationText: Carbohydrate counting helps regulate blood glucose levels and is a key component of diabetes management.

questions.questionText: Which lifestyle intervention should be recommended to a client with diabetes who is planning a pregnancy?
questions.explanationText: Regular exercise improves insulin sensitivity and supports healthy blood glucose control before and during pregnancy.

questions.questionText: A nurse is teaching a client with type 2 diabetes who is planning a pregnancy. Which two interventions should the nurse recommend? Select two.
questions.explanationText: Carbohydrate counting and regular exercise are effective interventions for improving glucose control and reducing fetal risks.

questions.questionText: Maternal hyperglycemia during organogenesis places the fetus at highest risk for which outcome?
questions.explanationText: Uncontrolled diabetes during the first trimester, when organs are developing, significantly increases the risk of congenital anomalies.

questions.questionText: The nurse explains that the period of greatest risk for diabetes-related fetal cardiac defects occurs during which stage?
questions.explanationText: The fetal heart develops during the early first trimester, making this a critical period for maintaining normal maternal blood glucose levels.

questions.questionText: Which nursing teaching point is most important during preconception counseling for a client with diabetes?
options: Achieve optimal blood glucose control before conception
Wait until the third trimester to monitor blood sugars
questions.correctAnswer: Achieve optimal blood glucose control before conception
questions.explanationText: Optimal blood glucose control before conception is one of the most effective ways to reduce the risk of congenital anomalies and adverse pregnancy outcomes.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes gestational hypertension?
options: New-onset hypertension after 20 weeks without proteinuria or severe features
Hypertension that occurs only during labor
questions.correctAnswer: New-onset hypertension after 20 weeks without proteinuria or severe features
questions.explanationText: Gestational hypertension is diagnosed when blood pressure is elevated after 20 weeks gestation without proteinuria or severe features of preeclampsia.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes preeclampsia?
options: Hypertension after 20 weeks with proteinuria or signs of organ dysfunction
questions.correctAnswer: Hypertension after 20 weeks with proteinuria or signs of organ dysfunction
questions.explanationText: Preeclampsia involves elevated blood pressure after 20 weeks gestation along with proteinuria or evidence of organ damage.

questions.questionText: How is chronic hypertension defined during pregnancy?
options: Hypertension present before pregnancy or before 20 weeks gestation
questions.correctAnswer: Hypertension present before pregnancy or before 20 weeks gestation
questions.explanationText: Chronic hypertension exists before pregnancy or is diagnosed before 20 weeks gestation.

questions.questionText: At what gestational age do gestational hypertension and preeclampsia typically develop?
questions.explanationText: Both gestational hypertension and preeclampsia are pregnancy-specific conditions that develop after 20 weeks gestation.

questions.questionText: Which finding distinguishes preeclampsia from gestational hypertension?
options: Blood pressure greater than 140/90 mm Hg
questions.explanationText: Proteinuria or evidence of organ dysfunction differentiates preeclampsia from gestational hypertension.

questions.questionText: Which blood pressure reading meets the criteria for severe hypertension in pregnancy?
questions.explanationText: A blood pressure of 160/110 mm Hg or higher is considered a severe feature of hypertensive disorders in pregnancy.

questions.questionText: Which symptom is most concerning for severe preeclampsia?
questions.explanationText: Persistent severe headache may indicate cerebral involvement and is a severe feature of preeclampsia.

questions.questionText: Which assessment finding may indicate worsening preeclampsia?
questions.explanationText: Blurred vision, flashing lights, or visual disturbances may indicate cerebral vasospasm and worsening preeclampsia.

questions.questionText: A patient with mild gestational hypertension is being managed at home. Which intervention would the nurse expect?
questions.explanationText: Mild gestational hypertension may be managed with close maternal and fetal monitoring, including blood pressure assessments.

questions.questionText: Which fetal assessment is commonly used to monitor fetal well-being in patients with hypertensive disorders of pregnancy?
questions.explanationText: NSTs are commonly used to monitor fetal oxygenation and well-being in pregnancies complicated by hypertension.

questions.questionText: Which medication is commonly administered to prevent seizures in severe preeclampsia?
questions.explanationText: Magnesium sulfate is used to prevent seizures in patients with severe preeclampsia.

questions.questionText: What is the primary purpose of magnesium sulfate in preeclampsia?
questions.explanationText: Magnesium sulfate is the medication of choice for seizure prophylaxis in severe preeclampsia.

questions.questionText: A patient with severe preeclampsia is most likely to require which level of care?
questions.explanationText: Severe preeclampsia often requires hospitalization for intensive maternal and fetal assessment.

questions.questionText: Which nursing assessment is a priority when administering magnesium sulfate?
questions.explanationText: Loss of deep tendon reflexes may indicate magnesium toxicity and requires immediate attention.

questions.questionText: Which antidote should be readily available when administering magnesium sulfate?
questions.explanationText: Calcium gluconate is the antidote used to reverse magnesium sulfate toxicity.

questions.questionText: Which complication can occur if preeclampsia progresses and seizures develop?
questions.explanationText: Eclampsia is defined as the occurrence of seizures in a patient with preeclampsia.

questions.questionText: Which intervention is more likely for severe preeclampsia than mild preeclampsia?
questions.explanationText: Severe preeclampsia requires seizure prophylaxis with magnesium sulfate, whereas mild disease may be managed with monitoring alone.

questions.questionText: Which maternal assessment findings are considered severe features of preeclampsia? Select all that apply.
questions.correctAnswer: Blood pressure 160/110 mm Hg, Visual disturbances, Severe headache, Right upper quadrant pain
questions.explanationText: Severe features include severe hypertension, persistent headache, visual disturbances, and right upper quadrant or epigastric pain indicating possible liver involvement.

questions.questionText: Client is to undergo a biophysical profile.  What is being evaluated?
options: Fetal breathing, fetal motion, amniotic fluid volume
questions.correctAnswer: Fetal breathing, fetal motion, amniotic fluid volume

questions.questionText: Client is in labor and has epidural anesthesia block.   Client BP is 80/40 mm Hg and fetal heart rate is 140/min.  Which is priority?
questions.explanationText: Based on Maslow’s hierarchy of needs, the client should be moved to a lateral position or a pillow placed under one of the client’s hips to relieve pressure on the inferior vena cava and improve the blood pressure.

questions.questionText: Nurse in prenatal clinic is teaching amniocentesis, scheduled at 15 weeks of gestation.  Which should be taught?
options: This test requires the presence fo amniotic fluid
questions.correctAnswer: This test requires the presence fo amniotic fluid
questions.explanationText: Amniocentesis requires adequate amniotic fluid for testing, which is not available until after 14 weeks of gestation. Amniocentesis is a prenatal diagnostic test in which a healthcare provider inserts a thin needle through the mother's abdomen into the uterus and removes a small sample of amniotic fluid (the fluid surrounding the baby).  The amniotic fluid contains fetal cells and chemicals that can be analyzed to: Diagnose chromosomal abnormalities such as Down syndrome, Detect genetic disorders (such as cystic fibrosis or sickle cell disease), Identify certain neural tube defects, such as Spina bifida, Assess fetal lung maturity in some late-pregnancy situations, Detect fetal infections in special circumstances

questions.questionText: Client is having nonstress test perfomed.  Fetal heart rate is 130 to 150/min, but. there has been no fetal movement for 15 minutes.  Which should the nurse do?
options: Immediately report situation to client provider and prepare for labor induction
Encourage client to walk around without the monitoring unit for 10 minutes, then resume monitoring
Offer client a snack of orange juice and crackers
Turn clinet onto her left side
questions.correctAnswer: Offer client a snack of orange juice and crackers
questions.explanationText: A nonstress test depends upon fetal movement, and this fetus is most likely asleep. Most fetuses are more active after meals due to the increase in the mother's blood sugar. Giving the mother a snack will promote fetal movement.

questions.questionText: Type 1 and type 2 pregestational diabetes mellitus (PDM).  Which is true?
options: Fetus is at risk of birth defects and congenital malformations
questions.correctAnswer: The fetus will be at risk of birth defects and congenital malformations

questions.questionText: Client at 28 weeks gestation has gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM).  Which is true?
options: I should include high fiber foods in my diet to stabilize my blood glucose; I will exercise moderately for 30 minutes a day, five days each week; If diet and exercise do not stabilize my blood glucose, I may need medication.
questions.correctAnswer: I should include high fiber foods in my diet to stabilize my blood glucose; I will exercise moderately for 30 minutes a day, five days each week; If diet and exercise do not stabilize my blood glucose, I may need medication.
questions.explanationText: Carbohydrate intake should not be greater than 40% of daily calories; clients should monitor their blood glucose level 4 times per day

questions.questionText: Client at 30 weeks has BP 160/116 and 4 hours previously 164/114.  Client reportrs blurred vision and persistent frontal headache.  Which complication of gestation is suspected?
questions.explanationText: Clients will present with a blood pressure of greater than or equal to 160 mm Hg systolic and greater than or equal to 110 mm Hg diastolic at least 4 hr apart after 20 weeks of gestation unless antihypertensive therapy has been initiated. The client's blood pressure and presenting manifestations meet the criteria for preeclampsia with severe features.;  Clients at greater than or equal to 20 weeks of gestation with blood pressure readings greater than or equal to 140/90 mm Hg on two occasions at least 4 hr apart with no other manifestations of disease will be diagnosed with preeclampsia without severe features.;  Clients with gestational hypertension do not present with the manifestations of worsening disease, such as unresolved headache

questions.questionText: Client in labor with oxytocin administrered.  Contractions occur every 2 min, last 90 sec, strong to palpation.  Baseline fetal heart rate 150/min, with uniform decelerations beginning at the peak of ocntraction and return to baseline after contraction is over.  Which action should nurse take?
questions.correctAnswer: Discontinue the oxytocin infusion immediately if a client is experiencing late decelerations due to uterine hyperstimulation.

questions.questionText: Client is 12 hours postpartuma nd received spinal anesthesia for cesarean birth.  Which requires immediate intervention by nurse?
Nurse reviews obstetric and medical records of clients attending prenatal appointments.  Which should nurse identify as at risk for eclampsia
options: Client is 42 years old; Twin pregnancy; client has multiple sclerosis; became pregant using in vitro fertilization
questions.correctAnswer: Client is 42 years old; Twin pregnancy; client has multiple sclerosis; became pregant using in vitro fertilization

questions.questionText: Clinet in labor.  Which reflects application of gate control theory of pain?
options: Turn client onto her left side
Encourage client to rest between contractions
questions.explanationText: The gate control theory of pain is based on the concept of blocking or preventing the transmission of pain signals to the brain by using distraction techniques such as massage. Massaging the client’s back focuses on neuromuscular and cognitive changes.

questions.questionText: Nurse in ER admits client at 40 weeks gestation, has ruptured membranes, and nurse observes newborn's head is crowning.  Client tells nurse she wants to push.  What should nurse say?
options: You should go ahead nad push to assist the delivery
You should try to pant as delivery proceeds
You should try to perform slow-paced breathing
You should take a deep, cleansing breath and breathe naturally
questions.correctAnswer: You should try to pant as delivery proceeds
questions.explanationText: Panting allows uterine forces to expel the fetus and permits controlled muscle expansion to avoid rapid expulsion of the fetal head.

questions.questionText: Nurse is teaching client at 15 weeks gestation and is to undergo amniocentesis.  Nurse explains the purpose of this test is to identify which conditions?
options: Anomalies in fetal chromosomes; Neural tube defects; Fetal gender
questions.correctAnswer: Anomalies in fetal chromosomes; Neural tube defects; Fetal gender
questions.explanationText: An indirect Coombs test is a screening tool for Rh incompatibility.;  Abdominal ultrasonography is used to identify cephalopelvic disproportion later in pregnancy.;  Examination of amniotic fluid yields data about genetic anomalies, such as hemophilia and inborn metabolic disorders. Examination of alpha fetoprotein levels in amniotic fluid confirms the presence of a neural tube defect, such as spina bifida. Karyotyping of fetal cells obtained from amniotic fluid  permits the identification of fetal gender, which is important if an X-linked disorder is suspected in a male fetus.

questions.questionText: Client is in labor and prescribed oxytocin.  What should nurse teach?
options: Your contractions will become stronger and more frequent
questions.correctAnswer: Your contractions will become stronger and more frequent
questions.explanationText: Oxytocin is diluted with sodium chloride and administered IV via an infusion pump device to induce or strengthen uterine contractions during labor. The client who is receiving an oxytocin drip is closely monitored to promote a safe delivery and prevent maternal and/or fetal complications. The desired concentration of oxytocin medication is determined by the desired labor contraction pattern that should increase in frequency, duration, and intensity. The nurse closely monitors risks of continuous IV infusion of oxytocin to determine when to discontinue the medication. Risks include fetal distress (fetal bradycardia) caused by hyper-stimulation of the uterus compromising blood flow to the fetus.  Uterine contractions lasting longer than 90 seconds should prompt the nurse to discontinue the medication.

questions.questionText: A nurse is assessing a client who has preeclampsia with severe features and is receiving intravenous magnesium sulfate. The nurse notes a respiratory rate of 8/min with absent deep tendon reflexes. Which of the following actions is the nurse's priority?
questions.explanationText: Client is showing signs of magnesium toxicity.  Stopping infusion is first priority.

questions.questionText: Client 28 weeks gestations, has preeclampsia with severe features, receiving IV magnesium sulfate.  Which are indications for this medication?
questions.explanationText: Eclampsia is a severe, life-threatening complication of pregnancy characterized by the sudden onset of seizures (convulsions) or coma in a woman.

questions.questionText: A nurse is assisting in the care of a client at 26 weeks of gestation who has just experienced an eclamptic seizure. Which of the following interventions should the nurse expect at this time?
questions.explanationText: Once the client is stabilized, assessment of fetal status is warranted via continuous fetal monitoring. An eclamptic seizure is an obstetrical emergency, expectant management and antenatal steroid administration are not considered post-seizure despite gestational age, and the infant may be born preterm.

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client who is to undergo an amniotomy.  Which is priority?
questions.explanationText: Variable fetal heart rate decelerations and bradycardia can occur with an amniotomy as a result of umbilical cord prolapse or compression.  Cord prolapse necessitates an emergent delivery.

questions.questionText: Nurse in prenatal clinic is teaching clinet in second trimester and has new diagnosis of gestational diabetes.  Which indicates need for further teaching?
options: I will reduce my exercise schedule to 3 days a week.
questions.correctAnswer: I will reduce my exercise schedule to 3 days a week.
questions.explanationText: Increased exercise benefits the client and can result in improved management of gestational diabetes.

questions.questionText: Nurse is caring for clinet scheduled for maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein test at 15 weeks gestation.
options: This test assesses fetal lung maturity.
It assesses various markers of fetal well-being.
This test identifies an Rh incompatibility between the mother and fetus.
It is a screening test for spinal defects in the fetus.
questions.correctAnswer: It is a screening test for spinal defects in the fetus.
questions.explanationText: The maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein (MSAFP) screening test is used to identify suspected neural tube defects (NTDs) and abdominal wall defects. These include spina bifida, microcephaly, and anencephaly.    This tool is the basis for further testing, such as amniocentesis and specialized ultrasounds.

questions.questionText: The maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein (MSAFP) screening test is used to identify suspected neural tube defects (NTDs) and abdominal wall defects. These include spina bifida, microcephaly, and anencephaly.    This tool is the basis for further testing, such as amniocentesis and specialized ultrasounds.
A nurse is creating a plan of care for a 39 weeks gestation client and in active labor.
options: Monitor fetal heart rate every hour
Check cervix prior to analgesic administration
questions.correctAnswer: Check cervix prior to analgesic administration
questions.explanationText: Prior to administering an analgesic during active labor, the nurse must know how many centimeters the cervix has dilated. Administration too close to the time of delivery could cause respiratory depression in the newborn. Monitoring the FHR every hour is not frequent enough. Even for low-risk clients, most facilities’ protocols require monitoring the FHR every 15 to 30 min while the client is in the first stage of labor and every 5 to 15 min in the second stage (as long as the FHR has reassuring characteristics). High-risk clients require more frequent monitoring.

questions.questionText: Nurse in prenatal clinic is teaching clinet who has a new prescription for dinoprostone gel.  Which statement should nurse include in teaching?
options: This medication promotes softening of the cervix.
This medication is used to treat preeclampsia.
questions.correctAnswer: This medication promotes softening of the cervix.
questions.explanationText: Dinoprostone is used to prepare (or ripen) the cervix for the induction of labor in clients who are at term. Dinoprostone stimulates uterine contractions.

questions.questionText: Client is getting a nonstress test (NST).  At end of 30 min period of observation, nurse notes the following findings:  fetal heart rate baseline is 120/min with minimal variability and no accelerations.  There are two decelerations of 15/min in the fetal heart rate during a period of fetal movement, each lasting 20 seconds.  The result of the test is...
questions.explanationText: An NST that does not produce two or more qualifying accelerations within a 20-min period is interpreted as nonreactive.  Qualifying accelerations peak at least 15 /min above the FHR baseline and last at least 15 seconds.  An NST is interpreted as reactive if the fetus has a minimum of two accelerations in a 20-min period, each lasting at least 15 seconds and peaking at least 15/min above the FHR baseline. A positive test is one of the findings for a client having a contraction stress test (CST).  This result indicates that late decelerations occurred with 50% or more of the contractions, even if fewer than three contractions occurred in a 10-min period.  A negative test is one of the findings for a client having a contraction stress test (CST).   This result indicates that at least three uterine contractions occurred in a 10-min period with no late or significant variable decelerations.

questions.questionText: A nurse is reviewing the medical record of a client who has endometriosis. Which of the following findings supports this diagnosis?
questions.explanationText: Endometriosis is a chronic condition where tissue similar to the lining of the uterus grows outside the uterus, typically on the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and pelvic lining.  Infertility is a significant manifestation of endometriosis. Some clients may coincidentally discover they have endometriosis while seeking treatment for infertility.

questions.questionText: Client is in first stage of labor and using pattern-paced breathing.  Client says she feels lightheadeed and her fingers are tingling.  Which action should the nurse take?
options: Assist client to breathe into paper bag
Have client tuck her chin to her chest
questions.correctAnswer: Assist client to breathe into paper bag
questions.explanationText: This client is experiencing respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation. The client should be assisted to breathe into a paper bag or to cup her hands over her mouth to increase the carbon dioxide level, which replaces the bicarbonate ion.

questions.questionText: A nurse is assessing a client in labor who had epidural anesthesia for pain relief.  Which of the following findings should the nurse identify as a complication from epidural block?
questions.explanationText: Maternal hypotension is an adverse effect of epidural anesthesia. The nurse should administer an IV fluid bolus prior to the placement of the epidural catheter to decrease the likelihood of this complication.

questions.questionText: Client has paralytic ileum following appendectomy. Which should nurse do?
questions.explanationText: A nasogastric tube helps decompress the stomach and bowel in paralytic ileus to relieve distention and prevent complications.

questions.questionText: Dietary teaching to client with history of recurring calcium oxalate kidney stones
options: Drink 3 L of fluid every day
Take 3,000 mg of vitamin C daily
Restrict calcium intake to one serving per day
Eat 12 oz of animal protein daily
questions.correctAnswer: Drink 3 L of fluid every day
questions.explanationText: Most kidney stones form from dehydration. High fluid intake helps dilute urine and reduce stone formation.

questions.questionText: Client with abdominal distention has NG tube with low gastric suction. What is nurse priority?
options: Check to see if suction equipment is working
questions.correctAnswer: Check to see if suction equipment is working
questions.explanationText: Priority is assessment. The nurse should first ensure equipment is functioning before implementing further interventions.

questions.questionText: Nurse is preparing NG tube for gastric suctioning. Put these in order.
options: prepare equipment at bedside, measure NG tube, instruct client to flex head forward, instruct client to swallow, obtain x-ray, connect to suction
questions.correctAnswer: prepare equipment at bedside, measure NG tube, instruct client to flex head forward, instruct client to swallow, obtain x-ray, connect to suction
questions.explanationText: Correct NG tube insertion sequence includes preparation, measurement, insertion with swallowing, confirmation by x-ray, then connecting to suction.

questions.questionText: For peptic ulcer disease (PUD), which makes a protective barrier over the surface of an ulcer?
questions.explanationText: Sucralfate is the only medication listed that coats and protects the ulcer surface, forming a barrier against acid.

questions.questionText: Client has peptic ulcer, which is teaching?
options: Monitor for changes in color in stool such as dark or black-colored stool
questions.correctAnswer: Monitor for changes in color in stool such as dark or black-colored stool
questions.explanationText: Melena or black tarry stools can indicate GI bleeding, a complication of peptic ulcer disease.

questions.questionText: Client has peptic ulcer. Which is a risk factor?
questions.explanationText: NSAIDs decrease protective gastric mucosa, increasing risk for ulcer formation.

questions.questionText: Which is a sign of GI perforation?
questions.explanationText: A perforated ulcer causes sudden severe pain and a rigid, board-like abdomen due to peritonitis.

questions.questionText: Client needs cholecystectomy due to build-up of calculi or gallstones
options: Gallbladder became inflamed due to build-up of gallstones that are blocking common bile duct
questions.correctAnswer: Gallbladder became inflamed due to build-up of gallstones that are blocking common bile duct
questions.explanationText: Gallstones can obstruct bile flow, leading to inflammation and symptoms such as jaundice and pale stools.

questions.questionText: Client has cholelithiasis. Which should nurse expect?
options: Left upper quadrant pain radiating to left shoulder
Right upper quadrant pain after high-fat meal
questions.correctAnswer: Right upper quadrant pain after high-fat meal
questions.explanationText: Gallbladder pain typically occurs in the right upper quadrant after fatty meals due to increased bile demand.

questions.questionText: A nurse is assessing a client with suspected non-mechanical bowel obstruction (paralytic ileus). Which assessment finding should the nurse expect?
options: Hyperactive bowel sounds in all quadrants
Severe cramping abdominal pain with diarrhea
High-pitched bowel sounds with visible peristalsis
questions.explanationText: A non-mechanical bowel obstruction such as paralytic ileus or spinal cord injury results in loss of peristalsis due to impaired neural stimulation of the GI tract. This leads to absent bowel sounds and abdominal distention rather than the hyperactive or high-pitched sounds seen in mechanical obstruction.

questions.questionText: A nurse is teaching a parent about intussusception. Which statement best describes this condition?
options: A section of the intestine telescopes into an adjacent section, causing obstruction
The intestine loses all nerve stimulation, causing complete paralysis of bowel movement
Inflammation of the appendix causes localized infection in the lower right abdomen
The colon becomes narrowed due to chronic inflammatory bowel disease
questions.correctAnswer: A section of the intestine telescopes into an adjacent section, causing obstruction
questions.explanationText: Intussusception occurs when one segment of the intestine slides or 'telescopes' into another segment, usually the small intestine into the large intestine. This leads to obstruction, swelling, decreased blood flow, and can result in bowel ischemia if not treated promptly.

questions.questionText: A 2-year-old child diagnosed with intussusception has a hydrostatic reduction (water-soluble enema) performed. Which finding should the nurse report immediately before surgery proceeds?
questions.explanationText: Passage of normal brown stool indicates that the bowel obstruction has been successfully reduced by the hydrostatic enema. This finding suggests resolution of the intussusception and may eliminate the need for surgical intervention.

questions.questionText: An older adult patient is taking methylcellulose (Citrucel), a bulk-forming laxative, for chronic constipation. Which behavior is most concerning?
options: Patient primarily eats white bread and drinks low-fat milk.
Patient takes the methylcellulose three or four times a day.
Patient takes the medication with a few sips of water.
Patient does not promptly act on the urge to defecate.
questions.correctAnswer: Patient takes the medication with a few sips of water.
questions.explanationText: Bulk-forming laxatives such as methylcellulose require adequate fluid intake to prevent intestinal obstruction and ensure proper expansion of fiber in the stool. Taking it with minimal water increases the risk of impaction or worsening constipation.

questions.questionText: A patient with a history of multiple abdominal surgeries and an ileostomy is admitted with sudden onset abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Which type of bowel obstruction does the nurse suspect?
questions.explanationText: Sudden onset of symptoms is most consistent with a mechanical small bowel obstruction. Paralytic ileus is more associated with recent surgery or immobility and presents with absent bowel motility rather than acute colicky pain.

questions.questionText: For a patient with small bowel obstruction, which order should the nurse anticipate?
options: Gastrostomy tube for intermittent tube feeding
Jejunostomy tube for continuous tube feeding
questions.explanationText: An NG tube is used for gastric decompression in small bowel obstruction to remove fluid and gas, reduce distention, and prevent vomiting and aspiration.

questions.questionText: The RN is supervising a nursing student performing an abdominal assessment on a patient with bowel obstruction. For which actions should the nurse intervene? (Select all that apply)
options: Palpate for abdominal distention using the index fingertip
Auscultate bowel sounds with NG tube connected to low wall suction
Perform physical assessment before asking about pain
Check skin turgor over the lower abdomen
questions.correctAnswer: Palpate for abdominal distention using the index fingertip; Auscultate bowel sounds with NG tube connected to low wall suction; Perform physical assessment before asking about pain; Check skin turgor over the lower abdomen
questions.explanationText: The nurse should use fingertips or hands (not a single finger) for palpation, turn off NG suction before auscultation, assess pain before physical examination, and assess skin turgor at more appropriate sites such as the forearm or chest rather than the abdomen.

questions.questionText: After 3 days of gastric decompression and IV fluids for a bowel obstruction, which findings are concerning? (Select all that apply)
questions.correctAnswer: Potassium 2.8 mEq/L; No ileostomy output for 2 days
questions.explanationText: Hypokalemia is dangerous and can worsen ileus and cardiac function. Lack of ileostomy output suggests ongoing obstruction or bowel dysfunction. Adequate urine output and stable blood pressure are not concerning in this context.

questions.questionText: The patient with worsening bowel obstruction is not improving after gastric decompression. Which order does the nurse anticipate?
options: Total parenteral nutrition and NG tube to suction
questions.correctAnswer: Total parenteral nutrition and NG tube to suction
questions.explanationText: When the GI tract cannot be used due to obstruction, the patient requires bowel rest with NG decompression and nutritional support via TPN.

questions.questionText: A patient is ordered potassium infusion (20 mEq in 50 mL over 2 hours). Which additional information should the nurse provide to the provider before administration?
questions.explanationText: Potassium is excreted by the kidneys, so adequate urine output is essential before and during infusion to prevent hyperkalemia and toxicity.

questions.questionText: Which medication order for a patient with bowel obstruction should the nurse question?
options: Polyethylene glycol-electrolyte solution for bowel prep
questions.explanationText: Laxatives are contraindicated in bowel obstruction because they stimulate bowel activity and can worsen perforation risk.

questions.questionText: What is the most common cause of peptic ulcer disease?
questions.explanationText: H. pylori infection damages the gastric mucosa and is the most common cause of peptic ulcer disease.

questions.questionText: What are the major complications of peptic ulcer disease?
questions.explanationText: These are the three major life-threatening complications of peptic ulcer disease.

questions.questionText: When a peptic ulcer perforates, which cavity does gastric contents enter?
questions.explanationText: Perforation allows gastric contents to spill into the sterile peritoneal cavity, causing peritonitis.

questions.questionText: If a patient is NPO and on gastric suction, what must always be included in their care?
questions.explanationText: Patients on NPO status with NG suction require IV fluids to maintain hydration and electrolyte balance.

questions.questionText: What is the action of sucralfate in peptic ulcer disease?
options: Provides a physical protective coating for peptic ulcers
questions.correctAnswer: Provides a physical protective coating for peptic ulcers
questions.explanationText: Sucralfate forms a protective barrier over ulcer sites to protect them from acid and promote healing.

questions.questionText: What is the action of misoprostol (Cytotec) in peptic ulcer disease?
options: Decreases acid secretion and provides mucosal protection; contraindicated in pregnancy
questions.correctAnswer: Decreases acid secretion and provides mucosal protection; contraindicated in pregnancy
questions.explanationText: Misoprostol is a prostaglandin analog that reduces gastric acid secretion and protects the mucosa but can cause uterine contractions.

questions.questionText: In peptic ulcer surgery, what is the difference between Billroth I and Billroth II procedures?
options: Billroth I connects stomach to duodenum; Billroth II connects stomach to jejunum
questions.correctAnswer: Billroth I connects stomach to duodenum; Billroth II connects stomach to jejunum
questions.explanationText: Billroth I involves partial gastrectomy with connection to the duodenum. Billroth II connects the stomach to the jejunum.

questions.questionText: Which nursing assessment findings support the patient’s probable diagnosis of cholecystitis?
options: Jaundice; Pain is worse after meals and when lying down; Patient reports indigestion; WBC count 12000/mm3; Intermittent right shoulder pain.
questions.correctAnswer: Jaundice; Pain is worse after meals and when lying down; Patient reports indigestion; WBC count 12000/mm3; Intermittent right shoulder pain.
questions.explanationText: Jaundice can occur when bile flow is obstructed due to gallbladder inflammation or a blocked bile duct, leading to accumulation of bilirubin in the blood.

questions.questionText: What is contraindicated for a client after a cholecystectomy?
options: Clamp T-tube to prevent loss of bile. Maintain patient in bedrest in supine position.
questions.correctAnswer: Clamp T-tube to prevent loss of bile. Maintain patient in bedrest in supine position.
questions.explanationText: Clamping a T-tube too early or maintaining unnecessary strict supine bedrest can interfere with bile drainage and increase the risk of complications such as bile backup.

questions.questionText: A client has nephrolithiasis (kidney stones). What chronic condition is most significant to his acute condition?
questions.explanationText: Gouty arthritis is associated with elevated uric acid levels, which can contribute to the formation of uric acid kidney stones.

questions.questionText: Which discharge instructions should the nurse anticipate for a client undergoing lithotripsy?
options: Increase fluid intake. Follow up with a nephrologist. Use a urine strainer. Medication instructions.
questions.correctAnswer: Increase fluid intake. Follow up with a nephrologist. Use a urine strainer. Medication instructions.
questions.explanationText: After lithotripsy, clients are encouraged to increase fluids to flush stone fragments, use a urine strainer to monitor passage, and follow up for ongoing management. Dietary changes are typically not the immediate priority until stone analysis is completed.

questions.questionText: Gout and kidney stones are both caused by...
questions.explanationText: Both gout and certain types of kidney stones are caused by the crystallization of uric acid in tissues or the urinary tract.

questions.questionText: What dietary substance should the nurse instruct the client to reduce or eliminate as much as possible for kidney stone prevention?
questions.explanationText: Purines break down into uric acid in the body, increasing the risk of both gout and uric acid kidney stones.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes lithotripsy?
options: Lithotripsy is a non-invasive procedure that breaks up kidney stones for easier passage.
questions.correctAnswer: Lithotripsy is a non-invasive procedure that breaks up kidney stones for easier passage.
questions.explanationText: Lithotripsy uses sound waves to break kidney stones into smaller fragments so they can pass more easily through the urinary tract.

questions.questionText: Which statement by the client taking allopurinol indicates correct understanding of the medication?
options: Allopurinol decreases uric acid levels in the body to prevent formation of uric acid crystals and stones.
questions.correctAnswer: Allopurinol decreases uric acid levels in the body to prevent formation of uric acid crystals and stones.
questions.explanationText: Allopurinol reduces uric acid production, helping prevent gout flare-ups and uric acid kidney stone formation. Tamsulosin helps relax the ureter, and naproxen is an NSAID that can increase bleeding risk or affect kidney function.
